title: Urgent clinics get shot in arm
full_text:
Federal Budget 2026 Urgent care clinics are here to stay and Medicare fraud is in the spotlight, while billions will be poured into public hospitals and Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme medicines.
These were some of the main budget decisions for health, which is set to make up more than 16 per cent of federal government spending next financial year, second only to social and welfare costs.
Medicare urgent care clinics have been allocated $1.8bn over the next four years – followed by half a billion dollars every year after that – in a clear sign the 137 care clinics will now be considered a permanent part of Australia’s healthcare system.
The biggest headline figure – and one the government was keen to spruik – was the $25bn in extra funding for public hospitals, which will bring the total investment over five years to $220.3bn.
The extra cash is not new and was agreed to back in January this year, under a hospital funding reform deal struck by the federal and state and territory governments during negotiations over NDIS reforms.
The budget also set aside money so Australians can access more medications on the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme, with $5.9bn to cover new listings.
There was also a four-year, $146.8m investment to crack down on fraud and “non-compliance” across Medicare and the PBS.
But, according to the government’s figures, the cost of this measure will be offset by the more than $670m in dubious Medicare and PBS payments that will be stopped by the Health Department as a result.
One of the biggest savings is the $3bn the government expects to make by removing the private health insurance rebate for people aged 65 and over, with the cash set to be redirected to aged care.
Some Australians will face higher out-of-pocket costs in select circumstances, with the extended Medicare safety net benefits to be capped at 80 per cent of the scheduled fee for a small number of healthcare services.
The government expects to claw back $43.4m over four years with this measure. Another small cut detailed in the paper was a lowering of Medicare rebates related to sleep disorders in children and adolescents, with the government budgeting for $3.4m in savings over four years as a result.
Other health initiatives detailed in the budget include $68.5m over three years in a bid to eliminate HIV transmission in Australia, and $379.4m over five years for a national medical stockpile to prepare for emergencies.
Cancer patients with an advanced or poor prognosis will benefit from a three-year, $71m investment to continue a cutting-edge program that provides comprehensive genomic profiling to identify potential clinical trials. More than $590m will fund key works on My Health Record, expanding its use across healthcare.
summary: The 2026 Federal Budget allocates significant funding for healthcare, including $1.8bn for Medicare urgent care clinics and $25bn for public hospitals, while addressing Medicare fraud and adjusting private health insurance rebates.
author: Sarah Booth
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: $1.8bn allocated for Medicare urgent care clinics over four years
$25bn extra funding for public hospitals, part of a prior agreement
New measures to combat Medicare fraud and changes to private health insurance rebates for seniors
relevance: These budget decisions impact healthcare funding and regulations, which are critical for actuaries involved in health insurance and public health policy.
